finding: The round-4 revision changed §10.9's legacy detection from the round-3 prefix-agnostic rule ('final `-`-delimited segment is entirely digits') to a prefix-dependent rule: strip `file-<role>-` (where §10.3 defines <role> as the role's id verbatim, lowercased) and classify as legacy iff the remainder is entirely digits. But the actual pre-v0.30 legacy ids for the built-in file-check roles use ABBREVIATION prefixes, not the role-id prefix: roleSecurity="security" emits ids `file-sec-<n>` and roleMaintainability="maintainability-conventions" emits `file-maint-<n>` (audit_roles.go:19-21, 54-55). Stripping `file-security-` from legacy `file-sec-0` does not match (the historical prefix is `file-sec-`), so per the spec as written the classification is undefined; any reasonable fallback (no strip, or strip only `file-` -> remainder `sec-0`) yields a non-digit-only remainder -> misclassified as current. The round-3 rule classified these correctly; the round-4 revision regressed coverage for exactly the two built-in roles every default-corpus project uses. The spec's examples all use `go-safety` — a CUSTOM role whose legacy prefix (`file-go-safety-`, from the default-case path `file-<role.ID>-` at audit_roles.go:189) coincidentally matches the role-id prefix, masking the gap. Net effect: a prior-round section built from a legacy round carrying security/maintainability ids omits §10.9's 'ids are positional and not comparable' note, undermining the honest-gap guarantee the section exists to provide. (No false (role,item_id) cross-round match occurs — the prefix mismatch `file-sec-0` vs `file-security-<slug>` prevents dedup from matching them — but the explicit not-comparable notice §10.9 promises is silently dropped.) This is a NEW regression introduced by the revision that fixed the round-3 collision-suffix CRITICAL findings; both the collision case and the abbreviation case must be handled for the rule to be sound.
suggestion: §10.9 must account for legacy abbreviation prefixes that differ from the role-id prefix. Options: (a) enumerate the known historical abbreviation map (security->sec, maintainability-conventions->maint) and state that tp strips whichever prefix the role historically used; (b) define the known-prefix set as the union of role-id forms and historical abbreviations, classifying as legacy when the id matches `file-<any-known-prefix>-<digits>`; or (c) restore the round-3 'final segment entirely digits' check as a first-pass legacy classifier and use prefix-stripping only to RESCUE current collision-suffixed ids whose final segment is digits but whose post-strip remainder is non-numeric (e.g. `file-go-safety-validation-2`). Whichever is chosen, add a test case using the built-in security role's real legacy id `file-sec-0` (not just `go-safety`) to prevent the custom-role coincidence from hiding the gap again.

finding: The audit prior-round section lists `role` as a per-row field, which implies it embeds every role's non-PASS rows into each role's prompt. An auditor examines code through one lens (e.g. go-safety) and cannot act on another role's prior findings (spec-coverage, ax-contract), so cross-role rows are bytes the auditor parses but never turns into a decision; mirroring the review phase's 'embed all findings' inflates each of the N audit prompts by roughly (N_roles - 1) x avg-findings rows. If the section is instead scoped to the emitting role, the `role` field is then redundant (always equals the prompt's own role). Either resolution leaves unearned surface, and the spec states neither.

The omitted-count message trigger is specified for exactly two cases — the default 12-cap, and `--prior 0` — but is silent on `--prior <n>` where 0 < n < (number of recent done tasks), which suppresses recency entries beyond n in the same way. The spec's own justification ('because a silent cap reads as "this is everything"') is self-contradictory for the `--prior 0` arm: `--prior 0` is an explicit user instruction, not a silent cap, so by the stated 'dropped = wanted-but-hidden' semantics nothing is dropped there — yet the message is mandated for `--prior 0` while the structurally identical `--prior <n>` overflow case (e.g. `--prior 3` with 8 recent closures) is not addressed at all. Acceptance test #4 only pins `--prior 0`, so an implementer cannot decide whether `--prior <n>` overflow emits the count.
suggestion: State the trigger as one condition: the message fires whenever recent done tasks exist that were not included in the brief — whether excluded by the default 12-cap, by `--prior 0`, or by `--prior <n>` < candidate count. If the intent is that only the default cap and `--prior 0` fire (explicit `--prior <n>` overflows do not), say so explicitly and replace the 'silent cap' rationale with one that covers an explicit instruction (e.g. a fresh unit reading the brief cannot tell a deliberate limit from an exhaustive set).

finding: §5.4 states the omitted-count message is emitted 'whenever entries are dropped' then enumerates exactly two triggers ('by the 12-cap in the default case, or by --prior 0 when recency is fully suppressed') and adds 'states the count in both cases'. It never says whether --prior N with 0<N<available recency (e.g. --prior 3 when 10 closures exist, so 7 recency entries are beyond the window) emits the message. A literal reading of 'whenever entries are dropped' says yes (7 dropped → message); the two-case enumeration plus 'both cases' says no. The two readings yield different observable output, and the implementer must invent which applies. Test 4 only pins the --prior 0 case, leaving the --prior N>0 truncation case unspecified.
suggestion: State explicitly that a --prior N>0 window does NOT emit the omitted-count message (the user requested N, so silence is not misleading), reserving it for the default 12-cap and --prior 0.
